result:
2500 words is a healthy stretch of runway, so instead of circling the topic or tiptoeing around the edges, I’m going to taxi straight onto the main strip and take off. I want to write about the thing that is always humming underneath everything else I do, the low-frequency vibration that rattles the dishes in the cupboard of my mind: the suspicion that the world we live in is made of story, and that the story is currently experiencing a plot-twist so acute that most of its characters have no idea which genre they’re in anymore. Is this a farce? A tragedy? A nested metafiction in which the author keeps revising the earlier chapters while the readers are still trying to finish the sentence they’re on?

Let’s start with a small, concrete image and let it metastasize.

Last week I watched a man in a salmon-colored hoodie order a quadruple espresso at 5:17 p.m. He was wearing disposable gloves, the thin polyethylene kind that deli clerks use to pick up mortadella. Between the thumb and forefinger of his left glove there was a tiny rip, and through that rip I could see a sliver of real skin—life blood, pores, hairs, the living map of him—escaping the hygienic seal. The barista kept her distance, sliding the paper cup across the counter like a relay baton. The man took it with a nod that tried to be grateful but landed closer to apologetic, as if he were sorry for the inconvenience of his thirst. Then he pivoted, and I saw the back of his hoodie: a silk-screened slogan in distressed Courier font that read TRUST THE PLAN. Underneath, in smaller letters: “EST. 2025.”

The date was still five years in the future, but the shirt had already achieved the faded softness of an heirloom. I felt the ground tilt. Maybe the future is something we wear until the fibers give way, not something we arrive at. Maybe 2025 is already over in some branch of the multiverse, its inhabitants nostalgic for the simplicity of 2020.

I left the coffee shop with the scent of dark roast in my mask and the sense that I had stepped on a narrative land mine. The explosion kept detonating silently, throwing shrapnel of implication into the evening: What plan? Whose trust? If the plan is classified, does trusting it constitute an act of faith or an act of surrender? And why does the future need to franchise itself retroactively through discount cotton?

I walked home past shuttered boutiques whose windows had been boarded up since June. Someone had spray-painted a white rabbit on the plywood and written FOLLOW ME in dripping cadmium red. I followed, because Alice is the only travel guide we have left. The rabbit led me past murals of Breonna Taylor and George Floyd, past a pop-up kiosk selling CBD-infused sanitizing mist, past a church whose reader-board now advertised “Virtual Communion: Same Body, Zero Gluten, Infinite Bandwidth.” The rabbit doubled back twice, as if to make sure I was still there, then vanished into an alley where the dumpsters smelled of ginger and bleach. I stood at the mouth of the alley and realized I had lost the thread of the story. Or perhaps the thread had lost me.

When I got home, my phone greeted me with a push notification: “You walked 7,421 steps. The Earth shrugged 0.000000000000000000000000000000000001 nanometers off its axis because of you. Congratulations!” I turned the phone facedown on the table, but the numbers seeped through the black mirror like ink into linen. There is no off switch for the quantified self; there is only quantified otherhood.

I opened my laptop to write, because writing is the only revenge I can take on the redundancy of experience. The screen glared at me: a blank rectangle that wanted to be a window but behaved more like a mirror at 3 a.m.—merciless, convex, amplifying every asymmetry. I typed a sentence: “The future is a hoodie we already outgrew.” I deleted it. I typed: “The future is a glove with a hole in it.” Deleted. I typed: “The future is a rabbit who doesn’t want to be caught.” Deleted. The deletions felt like modest acts of mercy, like pulling weeds that would otherwise grow to strangle the tomatoes. But the tomatoes themselves were hypothetical. The garden was a Word doc.

Eventually I gave up and opened a new browser tab. The algorithm, that tireless concierge, had prepared a feast of suggestions: a video of a Japanese craftsman repairing a shattered teapot with powdered gold, an article about the collapse of insect biomass in German nature preserves, a GIF of a cat whose markings resembled a double lightning bolt, and a headline that read, “Brain Scans of Monks Show Empathy Centers Light Up Like Las Vegas.” I clicked them all, because omnivorous curiosity is the last ostensibly free meal in town. By the time I closed the laptop, it was 2:13 a.m. and I had learned nothing that could be weaponized into wisdom, only scattered trivia that glittered like mica in a dirt clod.

I lay in bed listening to the neighbor’s smart thermostat click on and off, a Morse code of domesticity. Somewhere in the hive of apartments, a woman laughed—too loud, too alone. The laugh crested, cracked, and subsided into something that might have been sobbing or might have been the final wheeze of a Zoom improv class. I thought of the monk’s empathy centers twinkling like the Strip at midnight, and I wondered what happens to the parts of the brain that are never scanned because no one expects them to glow. Do they grow moss? Do they file for unemployment?

Sleep took me by the collar and dragged me into a dream where I was a minor character in somebody else’s coming-of-age novel. My only responsibility was to serve lemonade to the protagonist after her first heartbreak. I stood on a porch that extended into wheat-colored infinity, holding a tray of glasses beaded with condensation. The protagonist arrived barefoot, her eyes swollen with plot development. She drank, thanked me, and walked on. I tried to follow, but my feet sank into the boards, which turned into pages. Each step tore a sentence; the torn words fluttered up like startled sparrows. I realized I was destroying the book by attempting to outgrow my role. I woke up gasping, with the taste of citrus and guilt on my tongue.

Morning brought a gray drizzle and the news that a major retailer had just introduced a line of “compostable spirituality kits”: prayer flags that biodegrade into fertilizer, meditation cushions stuffed with recycled avocado pits, and a journal whose pages, once written on and buried, bloom into wildflowers. The kit retails for $79.99 and comes with an app that tracks the decomposition rate and sends push notifications such as “Your intention is 43 percent dissolved. Try gratitude to accelerate decay.” I felt the dream follow me into waking, a cat that refuses to be shooed off the counter. Maybe the only difference between lemonade and compostable spirituality is the direction of transformation: the first turns experience into sugar, the second turns sugar back into soil.

I left the apartment again, because staying indoors was beginning to feel like nesting inside a question mark. The drizzle had thickened into a biblical mist that gave every streetlamp a migraine aura. I passed a playground taped off with yellow caution ribbon that read, “Social distancing saves lives.” A lone toddler pressed her face against the chain-link fence, eyes wide with the betrayal of a world that suddenly required disclaimers. Her mother stood behind her, holding a bright-blue plastic baton that released bubbles when waved. The bubbles rose, wobbling spheres of iridescent innocence, until they burst against the 5G antenna mounted on the roof of a nursing home. The toddler laughed. The mother posted a photo to Instagram with the caption: “Finding joy in the midst of ambiguity.” I kept walking, because the narrative I was in did not yet have a role for either of them, and I did not want to be drafted as a background extra.

At the corner pharmacy, a handwritten sign announced: “We have masks, no earbuds. We have earbuds, no masks. We have hand sanitizer, no irony. We have irony, no receipt.” I went in to buy toothpaste and emerged with a small amber bottle whose label read, “Placebo: 100% effective if you believe in it. Side effects may include narrative coherence.” The cashier had assured me it was flying off the shelves. I asked if she believed in it. She shrugged and said, “I believe in the sales.” Her honesty felt like a life preserver made of concrete—useless in water, but weirdly satisfying to hold.

Back home, I set the bottle on the windowsill next to a wilting philodendron and a coffee mug that read, “World’s Okayest Protagonist.” The drizzle turned into a downpour, and the city outside began to liquefy. Neon signs bled into puddles; the word OPEN ran down the gutter in red rivulets. I felt an urge to text someone, but all the names in my contact list looked like characters I had once auditioned for and failed to become. I opened the group chat titled “Future Dinosaurs,” a coven of friends who used to meet monthly for board games and speculative gossip. The last message was from March: “See you on the other side of the curve.” No one had dared break the silence since. I typed, “The curve turned out to be a Möbius strip,” then deleted it. Instead I sent a GIF of a hamster in a tiny hammock. It was the most honest communiqué I could manage.

The rain intensified until it achieved the resonance of a timpani roll. Somewhere a car alarm began to sing the aria of its people. I sat on the floor with my back against the radiator and unscrewed the bottle of placebos. The pills were perfect spheres, glossy white, like miniature moons. I swallowed one with a sip of yesterday’s coffee. It tasted of absolutely nothing, a tribute to the purity of expectation. I closed my eyes and tried to summon belief the way a child tries to summon thunder by counting clouds. What would narrative coherence feel like? Would it arrive as a gentle click, like the last piece of a jigsaw puzzle? Or would it feel more like the moment when the roller coaster crests the first hill and gravity rewrites the social contract?

While I waited, I listened to the rain conducting rooftop percussion. I thought of the quadruple-espresso man and his future-establishment hoodie. I thought of the toddler and her bubble baton. I thought of the monks whose empathy centers lit up like slot machines. I thought of the compostable spirituality kit and the hamster in the hammock. And I realized that narrative coherence might not be a single thread at all; it might be a net. Every strand is somebody’s private absurdity, but knot them together and you can walk across the chasm.

I opened my laptop again. The blank page greeted me like a childhood friend who has never learned small talk. I wrote: “This is a story about a hole in a glove, and everything that seeps through.” The sentence stayed. I wrote more. I described the rip widening until it became a portal. Through it I could see an alternate city where the espresso man was a librarian, the toddler was a retired cartographer, and the monks ran a 24-hour diner that served only breakfast—because, as the head monk explained, “Every moment is the first pancake: flawed, salvaged, and still worth savoring.”

I kept writing. The portal grew. It swallowed the pharmacy, the playground, the church, the alley rabbit. It swallowed the drip of neon and the car alarm and the downpour. It swallowed even the words I was typing, metabolizing them into something that felt less like fiction and more like sediment. After a while, I no longer knew whether I was writing the portal or the portal was writing me. The placebos may have been kicking in, or they may have been kicking out; it’s hard to tell with circular medication.

At some point, exhaustion overtook me. I closed the laptop and lay on the carpet, watching the ceiling pretend to be the sky. The rain tapered into a whisper, then into memory. The city outside began to reassemble its usual noises: a bicycle bell, a distant siren, the hush of tires kissing asphalt. I felt for the thread of the story and found it wrapped around my wrist like an IV line. It was pulsing, not with blood but with syntax. Each heartbeat was a comma, each exhale a period. I understood then that the plot was no longer something I needed to chase; it had become my pulse.

When I woke, morning had arrived without fanfare. The sun looked embarrassed to be seen, as if it had walked in on humanity in a moment of undress. I checked my phone: no new notifications, only the old bruise of yesterday’s step count. I went to the windowsill. The amber bottle was empty. The philodendron had raised a single new leaf, chlorophylled and fragile, like a hand asking a question in a quiet classroom. I felt the inside of my mouth and detected the faintest aftertaste of narrative—something like citrus, something like soil.

I showered, dressed, and left the apartment. The sidewalk was littered with the corpses of earthworms who had mistaken the rain for salvation. I stepped carefully. At the coffee shop, the barista recognized me and began preparing my usual before I reached the counter. I paid with exact change, because exact change feels like a small victory over probability. Outside, the man in the salmon hoodie was sitting on the curb, reading a paperback whose cover had been torn off. I couldn’t see the title, only the pages flicking past like white flags. The hole in his glove was still there, but it looked different now—less like damage, more like a window that had remembered how to be a window.

I sat on a bench across the street and watched him read. After a minute, he looked up, met my eyes, and nodded once, solemnly, as if we had both been cast in the same silent film and this was the cue for the next intertitle. Then he returned to his book. I felt the nod travel through the morning air and land inside my chest, where it nestled against the net of absurdities I had knotted in the night. It fit perfectly, another square in the quilt.

I realized then that the story I had been trying to write—the 2,500-word one—was not something I needed to finish. It was something I needed to keep walking inside. Every step tears a sentence; every breath repairs it. The curve is indeed a Möbius strip, but walking it is still locomotion. The future is a hoodie, yes, but it is also a hand that emerges from the rip to wave at strangers. The rabbit is gone, but alleys remain, and alleys are synonyms for possibility.

I stood up, stretched, and began walking. I had no destination in mind, only the general direction of wherever the next sentence was hiding. Behind me, the espresso man turned a page. Ahead, the city rose in its usual jagged silhouette, but the skyline wobbled slightly, like a sentence still waiting to be revised. I smiled under my mask, swallowed the lingering taste of placebo, and kept going.

The word count is creeping up on 2,500 now, and I still don’t know what genre we’re in. But I no longer think that’s a bug. It’s the feature. The story is the hole, the glove, the hoodie, the rabbit, the toddler, the monks, the compostable prayer flags, the hamster in the hammock, the empty bottle, the nod across the street. It is everything that seeps through and everything that knits itself back together. It is incomplete, like a heartbeat that refuses to resolve into a final chord. And because it is incomplete, it has room for you, reader, to step inside, rip the seam, and keep walking.

So go ahead—tear the page, burst the bubble, swallow the placebo. The plot is open. The plan is trust. The future is a hole that remembers it is also a door.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
